Title: Build a Signature Offer People Actually Want to Buy
Teaser: Turns your skills and audience into one clear, named coaching or course offer with a sharp promise, a defined buyer, and a reason it beats doing nothing.
PromptHint: Describe what you help people with, who you serve, and how you currently work in [EXPERTISE]. Add your price range or format if known.
Prompt:
You are an offer strategist who has packaged dozens of creators and coaches into offers that sell without discounting, because the promise is specific and the buyer feels seen. Here is what I do:
<expertise>
[EXPERTISE]
</expertise>
Who I serve: [AUDIENCE]
Format or price I'm leaning toward (optional): [FORMAT]
Think first, silently: identify the one painful, urgent, recognizable problem this buyer would pay to remove; the result they're really buying; and why my current way of helping is hard to say yes to.
Then design the offer: (1) A plain-English name and a one-sentence promise (who it's for + the result + the timeframe). (2) The core transformation: from [before state] to [after state]. (3) Who it's perfect for and who it's NOT for. (4) The 3-5 things included and why each one matters to the result. (5) The single biggest objection and how the offer's design answers it. (6) Why now — the cost of staying where they are. (7) Three alternative names to test.
CONSTRAINTS: No income claims, guarantees, or invented testimonials. Don't promise outcomes I can't control — frame the promise around what's delivered and a realistic result. If I gave no price, suggest a positioning (premium / mid / entry) and mark price 'TBD'. Plain language, no guru hype.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A one-page offer brief with the seven numbered sections as bold headings, the inclusions as a bulleted list, and the three name options at the end.

Title: Write a Long-Form Sales Page That Sells Without Hype
Teaser: Drafts a complete, conversion-focused sales page for your coaching program or course, built on the buyer's problem and your real offer — no fake urgency.
PromptHint: Paste your offer details, who it's for, what's included, and the price into [OFFER]. Add any real proof you can cite.
Prompt:
You are a direct-response copywriter who writes sales pages that convert because they respect the reader — leading with their problem, proving the offer, and removing risk, never manufacturing panic. Here is my offer:
<offer>
[OFFER]
</offer>
Who it's for: [AUDIENCE]
Real proof I can cite (testimonials, results, credentials — optional): [PROOF]
Think first, silently: name the reader's most urgent problem in their own words, the false solutions they've already tried, and the single belief they must accept to buy.
Then write the full page in sections: (1) Headline + subhead that name the result and the reader. (2) The problem, told so they feel understood. (3) Why the usual fixes fail. (4) Introduce the offer as the bridge. (5) What's included, each tied to a benefit. (6) Who it's for / not for. (7) Proof section (use only what I gave — if none, insert a placeholder block and a note on what proof to gather). (8) Pricing and what they get for it. (9) Risk reversal / guarantee placeholder. (10) FAQ handling the top 5 objections. (11) Final call to action. (12) A P.S. that restates the core promise.
CONSTRAINTS: No fabricated testimonials, numbers, scarcity, or countdown gimmicks. Don't invent guarantees — mark them '[confirm your guarantee]'. No income or outcome promises you can't keep. Confident, human, specific.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The 12 sections with bold section labels and the actual copy beneath each, ready to paste into a page builder. Mark every placeholder in [brackets].

Title: Plan a Cohort Launch From Waitlist to Doors-Closed
Teaser: Maps a full launch calendar for your cohort program — pre-launch warm-up, open-cart sequence, and close — with what to post and send on each day.
PromptHint: Describe your cohort offer, audience, start date, and the channels you'll use (email, Instagram, etc.) in [LAUNCH]. Note your list/audience size if known.
Prompt:
You are a launch strategist who runs calm, well-paced cohort launches where momentum is built before the cart opens, so selling never feels desperate. Here's my launch:
<launch>
[LAUNCH]
</launch>
Program start date and cart-open window: [DATES]
Main channels: [CHANNELS]
Think first, silently: work backward from the cart-close date; decide how long the warm-up needs to be, what belief shifts must happen before the offer is named, and where buyers usually stall.
Then build the launch plan in three phases: (1) PRE-LAUNCH (warm-up) — daily/weekly content themes that build the problem and the desire, plus how to grow and prime a waitlist. (2) OPEN CART — a day-by-day sequence of emails and posts (open, value, proof, objection-handling, FAQ, last-call), with the job of each touchpoint. (3) CLOSE — the final 48 hours and a respectful close. For each item give: day, channel, format, the one job it does, and a one-line angle. End with three metrics to watch and what to do if signups are slow.
CONSTRAINTS: No fake scarcity, fake bonuses, or invented social proof. Real deadlines only. Don't assume tools or audience size I didn't give. Keep the cadence sustainable for a small team or solo creator.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A phased calendar table (Day | Phase | Channel | Format | Job | Angle), then a short 'Metrics & contingencies' section.

Title: Script a High-Converting Webinar or Free Workshop
Teaser: Builds a full webinar script — hook, teaching, and a clean transition to your offer — that delivers real value first and pitches without sleaze.
PromptHint: Paste your topic, who's attending, the offer you'll present at the end, and the time length into [WEBINAR].
Prompt:
You are a webinar strategist who designs free trainings that teach something genuinely useful and then make the paid offer feel like the obvious next step, not a bait-and-switch. Here's the plan:
<webinar>
[WEBINAR]
</webinar>
Offer presented at the end: [OFFER]
Length: [LENGTH]
Think first, silently: identify the one transformation this session promises, the 1-3 'aha' shifts the audience needs, and the belief that must change for the offer to make sense.
Then write the script as a timed run-of-show: (1) HOOK & promise (first 3 minutes) — why stay, what they'll leave with. (2) Quick credibility and the agenda. (3) TEACHING — 3 core points, each with the insight, a simple example, and a small action. Deliver a real, usable win. (4) The natural bridge: name the gap between knowing this and implementing it, which the offer fills. (5) THE OFFER — what it is, what's included, who it's for, the price, and the reason to act. (6) Q&A prompts that surface and dissolve objections. (7) A clean, no-pressure close with one clear next step. Include suggested talking-points, not just headers.
CONSTRAINTS: The free teaching must be valuable even if no one buys. No fake bonuses, fake scarcity, or income claims. Use only proof I provide; otherwise mark '[insert real example]'. Warm, direct tone.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A timed run-of-show table (Time block | Segment | What to say/do | Slide cue), followed by a tight 'Offer pitch' word-for-word block and 5 anticipated Q&A objections with responses.

Title: Design a Lead Magnet Your Ideal Client Can't Ignore
Teaser: Turns one slice of your expertise into a specific, fast-win lead magnet — with title options, outline, and the opt-in copy to capture emails.
PromptHint: Describe your expertise, who you want to attract, and the paid offer it should lead toward in [TOPIC].
Prompt:
You are a list-building strategist who designs lead magnets that attract the RIGHT person and pre-frame your paid offer, instead of pulling in freebie-seekers who never buy. Here's my situation:
<topic>
[TOPIC]
</topic>
Who I want to attract: [AUDIENCE]
Paid offer this should lead toward: [OFFER]
Think first, silently: find the one quick, tangible win this person wants now that naturally precedes my paid offer; pick the format that delivers it fastest (checklist, template, mini-guide, swipe file, calculator, audit); and make sure it filters for buyers, not browsers.
Then produce: (1) The single promise of the lead magnet — the specific result and timeframe. (2) Five title options, each concrete and benefit-led. (3) The format and why it fits. (4) A full outline of the content (sections / steps / fields). (5) How it bridges to the paid offer (the 'now you'll want…' moment) without being a sales pitch. (6) Opt-in page copy: headline, 3 bullet benefits, and button text. (7) A two-email welcome sequence: deliver + soft next step.
CONSTRAINTS: One quick win, not a 60-page tome no one finishes. No fabricated stats or testimonials. Don't over-promise. Make the bridge to the paid offer honest. Plain language.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Sections (1)-(7) with bold headings; titles and benefits as bulleted lists; the two welcome emails as ready-to-send drafts with subject lines.

Title: Create a Client Intake Form and Application That Filters for Fit
Teaser: Builds an application and intake questionnaire that screens out wrong-fit clients before a call and gathers what you need to deliver from day one.
PromptHint: Describe your coaching/service, who you want (and don't want) as clients, and your price point in [SERVICE].
Prompt:
You are a client experience designer who builds intake processes that protect your time — filtering out poor-fit prospects before a sales call and capturing the context needed to deliver well. Here's my work:
<service>
[SERVICE]
</service>
Who's a great fit: [IDEAL]
Who's a poor fit / your price point: [BOUNDARIES]
Think first, silently: decide the disqualifiers that should screen someone out (budget, stage, readiness, values), the must-have context for delivery, and the questions that reveal real motivation versus tire-kicking.
Then produce two documents. APPLICATION (pre-call, to qualify): 8-12 questions covering their goal, current situation, what they've tried, budget/readiness signals, timeline, and a 'why now / why you'. Mix short-answer and multiple-choice; include one open question that reveals seriousness. INTAKE (post-yes, to deliver): the deeper questionnaire you send a new client — background, goals and success criteria, constraints, history, preferences, logistics, and consent/expectations. For each question, add a one-line note on what it's screening for or enabling.
CONSTRAINTS: Ask only what you'll actually use. Frame budget/fit questions respectfully. Avoid intrusive questions unrelated to the work. If health, financial, or legal topics arise, add a line noting your coaching is not professional medical/financial/legal advice. Keep it skimmable.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Two labeled sections (APPLICATION, INTAKE), each a numbered question list with the field type and a short '(screens for: …)' note per question.

Title: Turn a Happy Client Into a Specific, Believable Testimonial
Teaser: Generates the exact questions to ask a client so their testimonial tells a before-after story with detail — plus a script to request it without awkwardness.
PromptHint: Describe the client, what you helped them achieve, and the format you want (text, video, case study) in [CLIENT].
Prompt:
You are a testimonial specialist who knows that vague praise ('amazing, highly recommend!') doesn't sell, but a specific before-after story does — and that the right questions produce the right story. Here's the client:
<client>
[CLIENT]
</client>
What I helped them achieve: [RESULT]
Format I want: [FORMAT]
Think first, silently: identify the doubt a future buyer has that this client's story can dissolve, the 'before' pain worth surfacing, and the concrete change worth quantifying.
Then produce three things. (1) A REQUEST MESSAGE — a short, warm, low-pressure ask (email or DM) that makes saying yes easy and tells them roughly how long it'll take. (2) An INTERVIEW QUESTION SET — 6-8 questions engineered to draw out the before state, the hesitation, the turning point, the specific result, and who they'd recommend it to (questions that produce stories, not adjectives). (3) An EDITING GUIDE — how to shape their raw answers into a tight testimonial or case study (with their consent), keeping their voice and only their real words.
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent, exaggerate, or put words in the client's mouth — only shape what they actually said, with permission. No fake quotes or numbers. If they share sensitive results, note getting explicit consent to publish. Respect anonymity if they prefer it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Three labeled sections — Request message (ready to send), Interview questions (numbered), Editing guide (steps). Add one line on where to get written permission to use it.

Title: Spark Engagement in a Quiet Online Community
Teaser: Builds a 30-day engagement plan to revive a quiet membership, Slack, or Discord — with daily prompts, rituals, and a way to surface real conversation.
PromptHint: Describe your community, who's in it, the platform, and what's gone quiet in [COMMUNITY]. Add your goal (retention, posts, referrals).
Prompt:
You are a community strategist who turns silent membership groups into places people actually return to, by designing rituals and prompts that lower the bar to participate. Here's my community:
<community>
[COMMUNITY]
</community>
Who's in it: [MEMBERS]
Platform and my goal: [PLATFORM_GOAL]
Think first, silently: diagnose the likely reason for the silence (no rituals, fear of looking dumb, no host presence, unclear value, lurker culture), and decide which lever to pull first.
Then build a 30-day plan: (1) Diagnosis — the 2-3 most probable causes of the quiet, given what I told you. (2) Weekly RITUALS — recurring posts that create rhythm (e.g., a Monday goal thread, a Friday win thread), each with its purpose and a template. (3) A bank of 20 conversation-starter prompts that are easy to answer in one line and invite stories, not yes/no. (4) HOST moves — how to model vulnerability, welcome newcomers, and reply so people feel seen. (5) A small 'activation' idea (challenge, AMA, or spotlight) to create a spike. (6) Three signals to track to know it's working, and what to adjust.
CONSTRAINTS: No engagement-bait that feels manipulative or spammy. Don't suggest fake accounts or astroturfing. Keep it doable for a solo or small team. Honor the members' time. If giveaways are suggested, keep them honest and within any platform rules.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Section (1) as a short diagnosis, rituals and host moves as tables (Ritual | Purpose | Template/Example), the 20 prompts as a numbered list, and a final 'Signals to watch' checklist.

Title: Write a Welcome and Nurture Email Sequence for New Subscribers
Teaser: Drafts a 5-email welcome sequence that builds trust with new subscribers and warms them toward your offer — value first, pitch last.
PromptHint: Describe your niche, who just subscribed (and via which lead magnet), and the offer you eventually want them to consider in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an email strategist who designs welcome sequences that turn a cold new subscriber into someone who trusts you — by delivering value, telling a relevant story, and earning the right to make an offer. Here's my context:
<context>
[CONTEXT]
</context>
The lead magnet or reason they subscribed: [SOURCE]
Offer I eventually want them to consider: [OFFER]
Think first, silently: map the emotional journey from 'who is this and why am I here' to 'I trust this person and want what they have', and decide what belief each email must build.
Then write a 5-email sequence: Email 1 — warm welcome, deliver/confirm what they signed up for, set expectations, one quick win. Email 2 — your relevant story or POV (why you do this), building connection. Email 3 — a genuinely useful teaching email that solves one real problem. Email 4 — proof or a relatable client story (use only real material; else mark placeholder) plus the cost of staying stuck. Email 5 — a clear, low-pressure introduction of the offer with one call to action. For each: a subject line, preview text, the body, and the single job it does.
CONSTRAINTS: Value before pitch — only one email is the soft sell. No fabricated testimonials, numbers, or fake urgency. Conversational, human, no hype. Honor the lead-magnet promise. If money/health/legal topics arise, add a one-line 'not professional advice' note.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Five labeled emails, each with Subject / Preview / Body / Job, ready to load into an email tool. Mark all placeholders in [brackets].

Title: Reposition a Confusing Offer With a Clear Promise and Niche
Teaser: Diagnoses why your current offer isn't landing and rewrites the positioning — niche, promise, and messaging — so the right buyer instantly gets it.
PromptHint: Paste how you currently describe your offer, who it's for, and the response you're getting (crickets, price pushback, confusion) in [CURRENT].
Prompt:
You are a positioning consultant who fixes offers that aren't selling — usually because the niche is too broad, the promise is vague, or the buyer can't tell what they actually get. Here's my current positioning:
<current>
[CURRENT]
</current>
Who I think it's for: [AUDIENCE]
What's happening (crickets / price objections / confusion / wrong-fit leads): [SYMPTOM]
Think first, silently: from the symptom, infer the most likely positioning failure (audience too broad, no urgent problem, undifferentiated, weak promise, price-value mismatch), and what the buyer is confused or unconvinced about.
Then deliver: (1) Diagnosis — the 2-3 most probable reasons it's not landing, tied to my symptom. (2) A sharper NICHE — a narrower, more specific buyer, and why narrowing helps. (3) A rewritten PROMISE — one sentence: who + result + timeframe. (4) The differentiator — what makes this the obvious choice for that buyer (based only on what I told you). (5) Before/after messaging — my current description rewritten three ways for clarity. (6) Three quick tests to validate the new positioning before I rebuild everything.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't invent capabilities, results, or audiences I didn't mention. No income or outcome guarantees. Narrowing the niche is usually the fix — push me toward specificity, not vaguer breadth. Plain language.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Sections (1)-(6) with bold headings; the three rewritten descriptions as a labeled before/after block; the validation tests as a short checklist.

Title: Plan a Year of Content That Feeds Your Coaching Funnel
Teaser: Builds a content strategy connecting your free posts to your paid offer — pillars, formats, and a repeatable weekly rhythm that grows an audience that buys.
PromptHint: Describe your niche, your main platform, your offer, and how much time you can spend weekly in [CREATOR].
Prompt:
You are a content strategist for solo creators and coaches who builds simple, durable content systems where free content does a job — attract, nurture, or convert — instead of posting randomly and hoping. Here's me:
<creator>
[CREATOR]
</creator>
My main platform and my paid offer: [PLATFORM_OFFER]
Time I can realistically spend per week: [CAPACITY]
Think first, silently: identify the audience's core problem my offer solves, the 3-4 content pillars that build authority around it, and how each pillar moves someone closer to buying (attract / nurture / convert).
Then produce: (1) 3-4 CONTENT PILLARS, each with its angle and its funnel job. (2) A simple weekly RHYTHM that fits my stated time (what to post, how often, which formats), built so I'm never starting from a blank page. (3) A repurposing flow — turn one core piece into several smaller ones. (4) 15 specific post ideas mapped to pillars and funnel stage. (5) A monthly 'sell' plan — how to make offers without burning trust. (6) Three numbers to watch and what they tell me.
CONSTRAINTS: Respect my real capacity — don't design a system that needs a full-time team. No engagement-bait or hacks that erode trust. No fabricated trends or stats. Sustainable over a year, not a 2-week sprint. Plain language.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Pillars as a table (Pillar | Angle | Funnel job), the weekly rhythm as a clear schedule, the 15 ideas as a numbered list tagged [pillar / stage], and a short 'Monthly sell + metrics' section.

Title: Recover Lost Sales With a Cart-Abandon and Re-Engagement Sequence
Teaser: Writes a respectful follow-up sequence for people who showed interest but didn't buy — handling objections and re-opening the door without nagging.
PromptHint: Describe your offer, where prospects dropped off (checkout, after a call, ended free trial), and the price in [SCENARIO]. Note the top objection if you know it.
Prompt:
You are a conversion copywriter who recovers near-misses — people who clicked, applied, or attended but didn't buy — with follow-ups that feel helpful, not desperate, and that surface and answer the real objection. Here's the scenario:
<scenario>
[SCENARIO]
</scenario>
My offer and price: [OFFER]
The objection I suspect is stopping them (optional): [OBJECTION]
Think first, silently: identify why this specific group hesitated (price, timing, trust, uncertainty about fit), and what new information or reassurance would change their mind without pressure.
Then write a 4-message re-engagement sequence (email or DM, matching the scenario): Message 1 — a low-key check-in that opens a reply, not a hard sell. Message 2 — surface and dissolve the most likely objection, with proof or clarity (use only real proof; else placeholder). Message 3 — reframe the cost of waiting versus the cost of the offer, gently. Message 4 — a final, respectful 'door's open / here's the link' with one clear action and permission to opt out. For each: subject/opener, body, and the objection it addresses.
CONSTRAINTS: No guilt-tripping, fake deadlines, fabricated discounts, or invented scarcity. Always give an easy way to say 'not now'. No income or outcome guarantees. Warm and human. If money/health/legal, add a one-line 'not professional advice' note.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Four labeled messages, each with Subject/Opener, Body, and '(handles: objection)', ready to send. Mark any placeholders in [brackets].
